one_line_takeaway: By reviving a classical (non-deep-learning) orthogonal/turbo-coded DFT watermarking scheme and adding perceptual masking, OF-SemWat embeds 1000-3000 bit semantic image captions into arbitrary-resolution AI-generated images with near-zero bit error under most processing and semantic edits (splicing, AI inpainting) where the learned baseline EditGuard fails outside its training distribution, at some cost in imperceptibility.
key_finding: On AI-generated images (Table 1, l=1000 bit), OF-SemWat achieves BER=0 on clean images and stays at or below ~5% BER across JPEG (QF=30,5), crop (10-30%), resize (0.7x,1.7x), rotation (5deg,10deg), noise (sigma=5,20), splicing (10-25%) and AI-inpainting (5-20%), with the single exception of JPEG QF=5 which spikes to 49.9% BER (severe quality degradation). EditGuard's BER is comparable only on clean images (0%) and on AI-inpainting/small-noise cases it was trained for, but is otherwise 16.1-68.5% BER (essentially unusable) and its LocAcc is 0 for every non-semantic processing case shown, confirming it generalizes only to distortions seen during training. Average PSNR/SSIM on clean AI images: 34.3 dB/0.92 for OF-SemWat vs. 37.5 dB/0.98 for EditGuard (OF-SemWat trades some imperceptibility for far greater payload -- 1000 vs. 64 bit -- and robustness generality). Results replicate on RAISE real images (Table 2, 1256x834) and on very large-scale RAISE images (2512x1668, Table 3) with payloads up to 3000 bit at s=64 (PSNR/SSIM 31.5 dB/0.92), with the expected robustness-vs-payload tradeoff shown by comparing s=64 (BER often near 0 but noisier under crop/JPEG) against s=128 (lower BER on strong crop/JPEG due to more redundancy). Table 4 shows LLM-based text error correction raises semantic similarity (SemSim) between recovered and original text from 0.25->0.58 at 15% BER up to 0.83->0.99 at 3% BER, with similarity around 0.9 already achieved once BER is below ~12%, indicating full semantic recovery is possible even with substantial residual bit errors.
core_contribution: The paper proposes OF-SemWat, a post-generation image watermarking framework (OFwatEnc/OFwatDec) that embeds a semantic textual description of an AI-generated image (e.g. the input prompt, or a caption produced by GPT-4) directly into the image at high payload (1000-3000 bits) and arbitrary/large resolution (up to 2512x1668 tested, targeting 1024x1024+ AI-generator outputs). It extends the traditional (non-learning-based) Quasi-Orthogonal Dirty Paper Coding (QO-DPC) watermarking scheme of Abrardo & Barni (2005), which uses orthogonal spreading and turbo codes, by embedding in the DFT medium-frequency coefficients of the luminance channel and adding two new perceptual-quality components: (1) a Watson perceptual sensitivity mask applied to host coefficients before embedding, and (2) a post-hoc variance-based perceptual mask that blends the watermarked and original luminance channels (Yw = (1-M)*Y + M*Yw') to suppress visible distortion in smooth regions. After extraction, an image-text consistency check compares the recovered description against the (possibly altered) image to detect semantic manipulation, optionally aided by an LLM (GPT-4) that performs bit-error correction on the recovered text.
threat_model: The setting is proactive/watermark-based provenance and semantic-tamper detection for AI-generated (and real) images. The watermark embedder and decoder share a secret key (spreading/shuffling pattern for the orthogonal codes), giving basic security against blind extraction by third parties. The paper's robustness claims cover two classes of post-hoc image modification an adversary or ordinary pipeline might apply: (a) non-semantic/incidental processing -- JPEG compression, resizing, rotation, cropping, Gaussian noise addition; and (b) semantic content modification -- traditional splicing (SegmentAnything-based region replacement) and AI-based inpainting (SD-XL inpainting guided by a SegmentAnything mask). The goal is not to prevent tampering but to survive it: the watermark should still be extractable (low bit error rate) after semantic edits so that the recovered text can be compared against the now-altered image to flag a mismatch. The paper does not model an adaptive adversary who specifically targets the watermark (e.g., watermark removal/forgery attacks, adversarial perturbations aimed at the DFT embedding, or knowledge of the secret key); robustness is evaluated only against generic processing and editing operations, not intentional watermark-defeating attacks.
limitations: The paper's own text flags: comparison is limited to a single baseline (EditGuard) because no other post-generation method can handle both large image sizes and high payloads simultaneously -- so the evaluation cannot show how OF-SemWat compares to other high-payload or arbitrary-resolution schemes. Imperceptibility (PSNR/SSIM) is measurably worse than EditGuard's on clean images. Robustness degrades sharply under severe JPEG compression (QF=5, ~50% BER) and moderately under strong AI-inpainting (up to ~12% BER at 20% inpainted area on RAISE images), i.e., high-strength attacks are not fully defeated. The semantic-mismatch detection step relies on an external LLM (GPT-4) for both generating the embedded description and for post-hoc error correction, introducing a dependency on a third-party proprietary model whose behavior/availability is outside the authors' control. The paper explicitly lists as future work: automating the image-text semantic-discrepancy check (currently informal/example-based, not a quantified detector), embedding richer descriptions, and better characterizing the robustness/payload/invisibility tradeoff. No evaluation against an adaptive adversary who specifically attacks the watermark (e.g., attempts watermark removal or forgery knowing the general scheme), nor any formal security/key-space analysis.

datasets_used: 100 images generated with Freepik Mystic (2048x2048, resized to 1024x1024)
100 real images from RAISE1k dataset, downsampled to 1256x834 and 2512x1668
quantitative_results: OF-SemWat clean-image BER = 0%, EditGuard clean BER = 0% (Table 1, AI images, l=1000 bit)
OF-SemWat BER stays <=4.7% across crop 10-30%, JPEG QF=30/0.7/1.7 resize, rotation 5-10deg, noise sigma=5/20, except JPEG QF=5 -> 49.9% BER
EditGuard BER ranges 16.1%-68.5% across the same non-semantic processing operations (Table 1)
EditGuard LocAcc = 0 for every non-semantic processing case reported (Table 1)
Clean-image quality: OF-SemWat PSNR/SSIM = 34.3 dB/0.92 vs EditGuard 37.5 dB/0.98 (AI images)
RAISE images (1256x834, l=1000): OF-SemWat PSNR/SSIM = 34.6 dB/0.95 vs EditGuard 35.7 dB/0.97; BER pattern mirrors Table 1 with JPEG QF=5 giving 50.1% BER
Large-scale RAISE images (2512x1668, l=3000 at s=64, PSNR/SSIM 31.5 dB/0.92): AI-inpaint 20% gives 9.9% BER at s=64 vs 11.6% BER at s=128 (l=1500)

Semantic similarity (SemSim, Sentence-BERT) before/after LLM error correction: 0.25->0.58 at 15% BER, 0.38->0.86 at 12% BER, 0.44->0.95 at 10% BER, 0.66->0.96 at 5% BER, 0.83->0.99 at 3% BER (Table 4)
baselines_compared: EditGuard (Zhang et al., CVPR 2024) -- learning-based proactive watermarking with 64-bit robust watermark plus semi-fragile localization watermark, 512x512 input resolution
